title.emphasis: Part II: Characterization of the Transaction Under Commercial and Labor Law
Section 3: Classifying Commissioned Artwork and Copyright Under the California Commercial Code (UCC)

section.para: The classification of the subject matter of the transaction—the artwork and its associated rights—is a critical preliminary step. Whether the transaction is governed by the California Commercial Code or common law has significant implications for the rights and obligations of the parties and for how a court will analyze the nature of the agreement.

title.emphasis: 3.1. Statutory Definitions: "Goods," "Future Goods," and "Specially Manufactured Goods"

section.para: Division 2 of the California Commercial Code governs transactions in "goods." Section 2105 provides the operative definitions. "Goods" are defined as "all things (including specially manufactured goods) which are movable at the time of identification to the contract for sale".25 This definition is broad, encompassing tangible personal property from manufactured items to growing crops.27

The UCC explicitly addresses items that do not yet exist. "Goods which are not both existing and identified are 'future' goods. A purported present sale of future goods or of any interest therein operates as a contract to sell".25 A commissioned piece of artwork, which will be created to a buyer's specifications and does not exist at the time of the agreement, fits squarely within the UCC's definitions of both "
future goods" and "specially manufactured goods".27 This provides a strong statutory basis for treating the physical artwork itself as a "good" subject to the provisions of UCC Division 2.

title.emphasis: 3.2. Intellectual Property in Commerce: Is a Copyright a "Good" or a "General Intangible"?

section.para: While the physical artwork is a "good," the intellectual property rights associated with it are treated differently. The UCC's definition of "goods" expressly excludes "things in action" 25, a category that encompasses intangible rights. Under Division 9 of the UCC, which governs secured transactions, intellectual property rights such as patents, trademarks, and copyrights are consistently classified as "

general intangibles".31 A general intangible is a catch-all category for "any personal property, including things in action, other than" specifically enumerated categories like accounts, goods, and instruments.32

Therefore, the sale of a commissioned artwork involves a transfer of two distinct types of property: the tangible physical object (the painting, sculpture, etc.), which is a "good," and the copyright, which is a "general intangible".31

title.emphasis: 3.3. Legal Significance of the Distinction: Application of UCC Division 2 vs. Common Law

section.para: This dual classification has significant legal consequences. A contract for the sale of "goods" is governed by UCC Division 2, which provides a comprehensive set of rules regarding contract formation (including the statute of frauds), warranties (express and implied), risk of loss, and remedies for breach.27
In contrast, a contract for the transfer of a "general intangible" like a copyright is governed not by UCC Division 2, but by the common law of contracts.27 While
UCC Division 9 provides the rules for creating and perfecting a security interest in a general intangible, it does not govern the outright sale of most general intangibles.31

This means that any agreement for the acquisition of a commissioned artwork is necessarily a hybrid transaction. The transfer of title to the physical object is a sale of goods under the UCC. The assignment of the copyright is a transfer of a general intangible under common law. This hybrid nature demands careful and explicit drafting. A contract that simply states the "sale of artwork" might be legally insufficient to transfer the copyright, potentially leaving the buyer with ownership of the physical canvas but without the legal right to reproduce, display, or create derivative works from the image. The agreement must contain separate and clear language effectuating both the passage of title to the "good" and the assignment of the intellectual property rights. This complexity also feeds directly into the primary challenge of avoiding a service contract classification. A court analyzing the transaction's "essence" may find that the true value lies not in the physical materials but in the creation of the unique copyright, pushing the analysis toward a finding that the contract's predominant purpose was the artist's creative service.

title.emphasis: Section 4: The Critical Boundary: Sale of Goods vs. Contract for Services

section.para: The distinction between a sale of goods and a contract for services is the legal battleground where the risk of employee misclassification is won or lost. If a transaction is properly characterized as a sale of goods, the strictures of California labor law may not apply. If it is characterized as a contract for services, a rigorous worker classification analysis is triggered.

title.emphasis: 4.1. The "Predominant Factor Test" in California Courts

section.para: When a contract involves both goods and services—a hybrid transaction—California courts apply the "predominant factor test" (also known as the "predominant purpose" or "Bonebrake" test) to determine the agreement's essential character.30 The court must ascertain the "essence of the agreement".30 If the primary purpose of the contract is the rendition of a service, with goods being incidentally involved (e.g., an artist painting a portrait, where the skill and labor are paramount), the contract is treated as one for services and governed by common law.30 Conversely, if the contract is fundamentally for a transaction of sale, with labor being incidentally involved (e.g., the installation of a purchased appliance), it is treated as a sale of goods governed by the UCC.30

Factors courts consider in this analysis include the language of the contract, the method of billing (e.g., lump sum for a product versus hourly rates for labor), and the comparative value of the physical goods versus the service component.35

title.emphasis: 4.2. Application to Creative Works: Analyzing the "Thrust" of a Commissioned Artwork Transaction

section.para: There is a notable absence of controlling California case law that directly applies the predominant factor test to a contract for commissioned art.38 This legal vacuum creates both risk and opportunity. The risk is that a court, as a matter of first impression, could easily conclude that the "thrust" of such an agreement is the artist's unique skill, talent, and creative labor. The paint and canvas are of negligible value compared to the artistic service that transforms them into a valuable work.30 This makes a standard commission agreement highly vulnerable to being classified as a service contract.

The opportunity lies in the ability to shape the outcome through meticulous contract drafting. Because there is no binding precedent, the language chosen by the parties to describe their relationship and the nature of the transaction becomes the most powerful evidence of their intent. A contract that is carefully structured to emphasize the sale of a finished "specially manufactured good" and de-emphasize the process of creation stands a better chance of persuading a court to apply the UCC.

title.emphasis: 4.3. Statutory Trigger: Defining "Labor or Services for Remuneration"

section.para: The entire framework of California's worker classification law, including the ABC test, is activated when one person provides "labor or services for remuneration" to another.39 This phrase, found in
California Labor Code § 2775 and Unemployment Insurance Code § 621(b), is the statutory gateway to an employment analysis.41

The Employment Development Department (EDD) and California courts interpret this trigger broadly. There is a default presumption that any individual providing labor or services in exchange for payment is an employee.41 The term "remuneration" is not limited to hourly wages; it encompasses any form of compensation paid for work performed, including commissions and non-discretionary bonuses.43 The burden then falls squarely on the hiring entity to prove that the relationship is not one of employment by satisfying the stringent requirements of the ABC test or another statutory exception.41

The classification of the transaction under the predominant factor test is therefore determinative. A finding that the agreement is for a "sale of goods" could potentially avoid the "labor or services for remuneration" trigger altogether, rendering the ABC test inapplicable. Conversely, a finding that the agreement is a "contract for services" leads directly into the statutory presumption of employment and places a heavy evidentiary burden on the hiring entity.

title.emphasis: Section 5: The Hobbyist Dilemma in California's Worker Classification Framework

section.para: The legal status of the artist themselves—whether they are operating as a professional business or engaging in a personal hobby—is a dispositive factor in assessing the risk of misclassification under California law. A failure to appreciate the distinction between tax law definitions and labor law requirements can lead to a catastrophic failure of any service-free contract model.

title.emphasis: 5.1. Defining the Artist's Status: The IRS Nine-Factor Test for "Hobby" vs. "Business"

section.para: For federal and California income tax purposes, the distinction between a business and a hobby hinges on the intent to make a profit.45 An activity engaged in for sport, recreation, or personal enjoyment is a hobby, even if it occasionally generates income. An activity conducted with a genuine profit motive is a business.47
To determine this intent, the IRS and the California Franchise Tax Board (FTB) use a non-exhaustive, nine-factor test. These factors include 45:

listitem.para: Whether the activity is carried on in a businesslike manner (e.g., maintaining complete and accurate books).
The expertise of the taxpayer or their advisors.
The time and effort expended in the activity.
The expectation that assets used in the activity may appreciate in value.
The success of the taxpayer in carrying on other similar or dissimilar activities.
The taxpayer's history of income or losses with respect to the activity (a history of profits suggests a business).
The amount of occasional profits, if any, that are earned.
The financial status of the taxpayer (a taxpayer with substantial income from other sources may be engaging in a hobby).
Elements of personal pleasure or recreation.

section.para: No single factor is determinative. An artist who paints for pleasure, does not keep business records, has never made a profit, and does not depend on art for their livelihood would likely be classified as a hobbyist for tax purposes.45

title.emphasis: 5.2. Intersection with the ABC Test: Can a "Hobbyist" Satisfy Prong C?

section.para: California's ABC test, codified in Labor Code § 2775, establishes a presumption of employment. To rebut this presumption, a hiring entity must prove all three of the following prongs 49:

listitem.para: (A) The person is free from the control and direction of the hiring entity in connection with the performance of the work.
(B) The person performs work that is outside the usual course of the hiring entity's business.
(C) The person is customarily engaged in an independently established trade, occupation, or business of the same nature as that involved in the work performed.

section.para: Prong C is the most critical in this context. The California Supreme Court in Dynamex clarified that to satisfy Prong C, the hiring entity must show that the worker "independently has made the decision to go into business for himself or herself".39 This involves taking the usual steps to establish and promote that business, such as through incorporation, licensure, advertisements, or offering services to the public or a number of clients.39

A fundamental and irreconcilable conflict exists between the legal definition of a "hobby" and the requirements of Prong C. A hobby is, by definition, an activity not engaged in for profit and not conducted in a businesslike manner.46 Prong C requires proof that the individual

is engaged in an "independently established... business".52 It is logically impossible for a hiring entity to argue that an individual whose activity legally qualifies as a hobby is simultaneously engaged in an independently established business.

title.emphasis: 5.3. Review of FTB and EDD Guidance: The Gap Between Tax Status and Labor Law Classification

section.para: An examination of guidance from California's tax and labor agencies reveals that they operate in separate spheres. The FTB's guidance on online activities focuses entirely on the nine-factor test to distinguish a hobby from a business for tax reporting purposes.45 The EDD's guidance focuses squarely on the ABC test and its exceptions for determining employment status for payroll tax purposes.41 There is no official guidance that directly reconciles these two standards or explains how an individual's tax status as a hobbyist should be treated in a labor law analysis.

This gap, however, does not benefit the hiring entity. The burden of proof under the ABC test rests entirely on the hiring entity. In a wage claim proceeding, an artist's own tax returns, where they have declared their art income as hobby income and have not taken business deductions, would serve as powerful evidence against the hiring entity. It would demonstrate that the artist does not consider themselves to be in an independently established business, making it nearly impossible for the hiring entity to meet its burden of proof for Prong C.

Consequently, the artist's status as a "hobbyist" is the single greatest vulnerability in any attempt to structure a service-free acquisition agreement. The legal and factual indicia that define an activity as a hobby for tax law purposes are the very same factors that would cause a hiring entity to fail Prong C of the ABC test. Therefore, a non-negotiable prerequisite for the defensible use of either of the contract models proposed below is that the artist must already be operating as a legitimate business, even if as a sole proprietor, and be able to provide objective evidence of that status.
